text: Phishing emails frequently impersonate trusted organizations like banks, IT departments, or government agencies. The attached malicious files are often disguised as invoices, shipping notices, or HR documents to entice the user to open them.

Spearphishing links direct victims to attacker-controlled sites that mimic legitimate login portals. The fake portal captures credentials when entered. Domains often use slight misspellings of the real organization (e.g., 'micros0ft-login.com').
Attackers use third-party services like LinkedIn, Twitter, or Slack to deliver phishing payloads. Legitimate-looking profiles message victims with malicious links.

Pages mimicking corporate single-sign-on portals harvest username/password pairs. Often hosted on free hosting services with subdomains like 'company-login.netlify.app' or 'sso-portal.000webhostapp.com'.
URL: paypa1.com/account/verify — typosquat replacing 'l' with '1'. Page mimics PayPal login. Asks for password and credit card. Classic financial credential harvesting.
URL: paypal-secure-login.tk/auth — uses suspicious .tk TLD plus brand name in subdomain. Common pattern: real brand name attached to attacker-controlled domain to fool casual inspection.
URL: rbc-online-banking-secure.com/login — Royal Bank of Canada impersonation. Domain registered within last 30 days. Asks for client card number, password, security questions.
URL: micros0ft-365-login.com — typosquat (zero instead of 'o'). Mimics Microsoft 365 login page including branding. Targets corporate credentials for use in BEC attacks.
URL: accounts.google.com.security-alert.xyz/signin — uses subdomain stuffing to put 'accounts.google.com' before the real attacker domain. Casual users see the trusted name first and trust it.
URL: amazon-account-locked.com/unlock — urgency-based phishing claiming account is locked. Asks user to verify by entering full payment details. Typically arrives via SMS or email.
URL: dhl-package-tracking-update.tk — fake DHL/FedEx/UPS tracking page. Asks for $1.99 'redelivery fee' to capture credit card data. Surge during holiday seasons.
URL: cra-canada-refund.com/claim — Canada Revenue Agency tax refund scam. Targets Canadian users. Asks for SIN, banking info, address. Common during tax season (Feb-May).

URL: http://198.51.100.42/secure-bank-login — login page hosted on raw IP address rather than domain. Legitimate banks NEVER serve login pages from raw IPs. Strong indicator of compromise or malicious infrastructure.
Pages requesting username AND password where the URL is on a freshly-registered domain (less than 30 days old) are high-risk. Combined with brand impersonation in page title, near-certain phishing.

URL: drive-google-shared.com/document/d/abc123 — fake Google Drive share notification. Clicking 'open' redirects to credential harvesting page. Often arrives via email with subject 'Document shared with you'.

URL: metamask-wallet-recovery.io/restore — crypto wallet phishing. Asks user to enter their seed phrase to 'recover' the wallet. Any site asking for a seed phrase is malicious — legitimate wallets never ask for it.
URL: docusign-secure-document.com/sign — fake DocuSign envelope notification. Targets executives and finance staff. Used to capture corporate credentials, then used for wire transfer fraud.

URL: legitimate-site.com/redirect?url=http://attacker.com — abuses an open redirect on a legitimate site to make the malicious destination look trusted. Hover-over preview shows the trusted domain first.

Phishing pages commonly use urgency language: 'Your account will be suspended', 'Verify within 24 hours', 'Suspicious login detected'. Combined with credential forms, this is a strong phishing signal.

IDN homograph attacks use Unicode characters that look identical to Latin letters but resolve differently. Example: 'аррӏе.com' (Cyrillic) vs 'apple.com' (Latin). Browsers display Punycode (xn--...) for these but URL bar copy-paste can hide it.
